topic: rant about broken X-ray machine at clinic

turns.content: the X-ray machine is down again. I am so tired
Oh no, that sounds incredibly frustrating, especially when you're trying to take care of patients. How long has it been down this time?

since Monday. four days. and they say the part has to come from Phoenix and it could be another week. meanwhile I have a patient with a possible wrist fracture and I have to send her to Gallup. that is a 3 hour round trip for her, you know. she does not have gas money for that

A week without an X-ray machine at a clinic that serves such a wide area is a real crisis. And asking a patient to make a 3-hour round trip for something that should take 15 minutes locally -- that's not just inconvenient, it's a barrier to care. Is there any way to get a temporary replacement or loaner unit while you wait for the part?

a loaner. that is funny. where would that come from. there is no spare anything out here. every piece of equipment we have is held together with prayers and duct tape. I am not exaggerating. the blood pressure cuffs are older than some of my patients

I believe you -- underfunded healthcare facilities often run on equipment long past its expected lifespan. It's not an exaggeration when you're working with what you've got and there's no backup. How are you handling the patients who need imaging in the meantime?

sending them out. Gallup or Flagstaff depending on what they need. I write referrals and hope they actually go. half the time they do not because they cannot get there. or they wait and whatever was wrong gets worse. then they come back to me and I still cannot see what is going on inside them

That's a terrible cycle -- patients who can't access the imaging don't get diagnosed, their conditions worsen, and then they're back with something more serious that's harder to treat. Are you tracking these missed or delayed diagnoses? Sometimes that data can help make the case for better equipment funding.

I keep a notebook. every patient I have to send out, I write it down. date, name, what I needed, where I sent them, whether they went. I have been keeping this notebook for years. pages and pages. nobody has asked to see it but I keep it anyway

That notebook is incredibly valuable even if nobody has asked for it yet. It's real-world documentation of how equipment failures translate into gaps in patient care. If the right person ever does ask -- or if you get the chance to present it -- that's powerful evidence. Keep writing in it.

oh I will. I told the regional office about the X-ray and they said they would expedite. expedite. that word means nothing to them, you know. it is just a word they say so you stop calling. I will call again Monday

Keep calling. Sometimes being the persistent voice is the only thing that moves things along in a system like that. 'Expedite' should mean something when patients are going without basic diagnostic imaging. Is Monday the earliest you can reach someone, or is there an after-hours contact?

Monday. nobody answers on weekends. the rez does not stop being sick on weekends but the offices sure do stop answering. I had a guy come in yesterday with chest pain and I could not even get a chest X-ray. I had to send him to the ER in Gallup by ambulance. that ambulance ride will cost more than fixing the machine

That's the cruel irony of it -- the cost of workarounds almost always exceeds the cost of just maintaining the equipment properly in the first place. An ambulance transfer for something you could have screened locally is an expensive way to compensate for a broken machine. I hope that patient is okay.

he is okay. it was not his heart, ahéhee. but it could have been. and next time it might be. that is what keeps me up at night, you know. not the machine being broken. machines break. it is that nobody seems to care enough to fix them fast when people's lives depend on them

That's the real wound underneath it all -- not the broken equipment, but the feeling that the people you serve aren't being prioritized. Your patients are lucky to have someone who loses sleep over this and keeps that notebook. Keep pushing. Monday, call again.
